sentences.text: Where is the young lady?
I don't--
Oh, I was wondering.
Do you have nothing to--
She has nothing to sit on.
There are lots of-- lots of pillows.
Why don't you take a pillow?
Really?
OK.
Hm?
Something?
What?
Oh.
I can put that over?
That would be nice.
OK.
Gisela Konopka.
G-I-S-E-L-A, and the last one is K-O-N-O-P-K-A.
But all my friends call me Gisa, G-I-S-A.
Berlin, Germany.
Mendel Peiper and Bronia Peiper, born Buttermann.
Now, that I have to spell.
Peiper is spelled P-E-I-P-E-R.
Mendel.
M-E-N-D-E-L.
And my mother's name is Bronia, B-R-O-N-I-A.
And the last name is Buttermann, B-U-T-T-E-R-M-A-N-N.
But sometimes it's spelled Pitterman, P-I-T-T-E-R, and the rest.
The, the kind of papers they got.
The-- they were immigrants from, at that time, Austria.
It's today, Poland.
And certainly, the papers were not always kept exact.
Well, when you talk earliest, I was born in Berlin.
It's a large building.
But I remember very vividly the outbreak of World War I.
I was a little kid.
I mean, I wasn't even four years old.
But we had a little store, and there was a person that lived in that same building.
[TELEPHONE RINGING]
Yeah.
My first memory-- I mean, there are other small ones-- but very important was the outbreak of World War I, and that's about 1914.
And we had a small store, and somebody came from upstairs, somebody we called uncle, but he was not an uncle.
And he yelled the word, mobilmachung, M-O-B-I-L-M-A-C-H-U-N-G.
That's the word.
It means, there's a war.
And I saw my mother crying.
I saw my father getting very white in the face.
I-- and then it goes on.
And I could tell a lot of memories about that.
For instance, that he had to go into the war, and I sat on a little chair and waved goodbye.
And Mother cried.
In the Austrian military, because Germany and Austria were in the same-- I think the Austrian.
Now, I might make a mistake.
And we were left alone with Mother, and she was a very young woman.
Mother had her first child when she was 19 years old.
So this is a very young family.
We are three little girls.
The youngest one was just born in 1914, Ruth, R-U-T-H.
Hannah, my older sister, is two and a half years older than I.
So we were a young family and she had to run a store, and she was very courageous.
And I could go, again, for hours about how she did it.
And how, as a small child, I saw her crying when she had to open difficult boxes.
But at the same time, she would do it.
I-- we were an enormously large family in Berlin.
I remember once when we were a little older, we counted.
We had more than 100 cousins living there, practically all of them were killed.
A few I have certainly have emigrated and are somewhere else.
But so-- but there was one family to whom I always felt very close.
The mother was my father's sister.
Her name was Mindzia, M-I-N-D-Z-I-A.
And her husband was my mother's brother.
And suddenly, I forgot his name.
It will come back.
But that means we were practically brothers and sisters.
And the father also had to go into the army.
So-- and the mother had very little.
We all had very little money.
Even that little store didn't have much money.
But the Tante, aunt Mindzia, was especially poor.
And there was lots of homework being done, you know, putting things together in the home.
And-- but those children lived very often with us.
There was really no room.
I can't describe all this in detail.
There is a store.
There are two very small rooms, one of that is always dark, all day.
The other one is light.
But the beds had to always be carried in the evening from one place to another.
I'm only saying all this because it's important memories.
But I have to say, I also want to say it, that too many people think that all Jews were rich in Berlin.
And in fact, I remember a Jewish refugee says, well, certainly, we were.
And I said, not we.
We never were.
But this family had a lot of meaning to me.
So these are very early memories.
There are many others I remember.
I-- maybe the things that I like to talk about are the things that have influenced my whole philosophy or thinking, even very young.
I was maybe, I don't know, maybe six years old or something like that.
Hannah was a little older, eight years.
And Ruth was much younger, so I'm, I'm in preschool, I forgot.
Now, to me, that's really exciting, that here's a baby, who is a Jewish girl.
And mother, who was Orthodox, if you want to, but not rigid ever, said, well, sure.
But only if you allow the two older girls to look at it and be there.
So here we were.
And I admired my little sister, sitting in that crib so quietly.
And all the angels around her.
[LAUGHS] And afterwards they said, well, anybody wants to say some poems.
And I was a showoff, I guess, all my life.
And I went right on the stage and said a poem.
My sister, Hannah, who is much more shy than I, never said a word.
I think I still have that in my memory because you will hear that in the rest of my story.
I never understand all this hate among religions, among the races, and so on.
I have to say that right away.
But it's a very early memory.
There are other little memories, oddly enough, mostly related to my mother again.
There is a woman who passes by and she stands still and says to Mother, oh, I am so grateful to you, Frau Peiper-- P-E-I-P-E-R-- because you helped me to accept my daughter in spite of what happened.
Well, I was very little then too, but I knew that she had a child out of wedlock.
That impressed me too, that one accepts anybody, and that was Mother.
Father was in the army, was sent back pretty early.
I can't tell you the year, because he actually had been-- always, he had angina and very serious.
And he was-- I-- today as an adult, I think he was an interesting man.
As a child, there was a mixture of loving him and being very afraid of him.
Loving him because he was very stimulating to me from the beginning.
He always walked with me around the corner and we discussed, I think, every problems of the world when I was 10 years old.
The house-- and we had-- I say house, the apartment was always filled with books.
And he told me that as a youngster, he was part of a socialist.
And he still was a socialist.
And he was persecuted in Poland.
He told me about the horrible treatment he got in his own family.
He was the youngest of the 12 children of his older sister, because his parents were dead.
Whatever happened, I don't know.
And so I felt very close to his history.
I felt with him.
We could talk to each other.
On the other hand, he was a man who was always very angry.
When he got mad, he would yell and hit anybody who was around.
I have often thought about it, how strange it is that I don't remember being hit very much.
But I still remember Ruth being hit, Hannah being hit, my mother being hit.
And he was yelling.
He was loud.
So this is my parents.
But it's-- I could tell for hours about them.
Mother told me about her story, because I was a curious child.
I always asked.
Otherwise, I wouldn't know anything.
I have to say, Mother came from Prczmyl, P-R-C-Z-M-Y-L.
Yeah, it's a difficult-- and she was-- they were poor, too.
But she was very proud of her family because her grandfather was a writer of the Torah.
And you had to be very good to do that.
But at the same time, she lost her mother very early and there was a stepmother who was very angry at having all these little kids running around.
And my mother was playing, because she liked balls-- she still-- I still never forget that.
She pressed the dry raspberries in her hands and threw them.
And they stuck at the ceiling.
And from then on she was forbidden to go to school.
So Mother had-- I don't know how many, a few years of schooling.
And I want to say that this woman, all her life, has read voluminously.
I still see her having books in her hand, almost all the time.
And she spoke fluently German, Polish, Ukrainian, and Russian.
And this is a woman who never went to school.
I am always a little amused about people, you have to go only to the university to learn something.
She-- I have to say that my father was born in Krakow, K-R-A-K-O-W.
And I don't know quite as much about him.
But as I said, he was very much mistreated in that family.
They took away his pants when they didn't want him to go out.
And the poor man, when he was dying, yelled for his pants because he felt that he needed them.
But I-- also, he had very little schooling, practically none.
And this is a man who, as I say, read every literature you could find.
I found when, when my mother had to emigrate much later, I found letters that he wrote to her about all the theater he visited in Berlin.
Oh, he loved the theater.
I still do.
And he was very knowledgeable about literature.
But again, no formal education.
And I could go more about that, but I think that's enough.
Yeah.
I have to say that.
My parents belonged to a small-- I'm-- Well, I don't even know what I should call it synagogue.
I should say schul.
S-C-H-U-L.
It was in a building, just a little place.
And went regularly, and I went regularly with them.
I was a very pious child.
I wanted to be.
I started fasting on the high holy days long before I should have to do it.
The store, you know, was a kosher store.
So I will not go into all the details, but at Passover, it was horrible because we had to change everything and we worked very, very hard.
Well, everything that was in the store had to be taken out, and only matzo was put in.
And everything was cleaned out.
But we children, at that time, had to work so hard because our clients that were richer Jews in the neighborhood would never come and pick up anything.
So we had to carry odd things.
I was known as a schlepper.
S-C-H-L-E-P-P-E-R.
I could carry, from early on, everything.
My hands were strong.
But it was very hard.
And at night, we had to prepare for the next day, to get things together.
And I, I still remember falling asleep on the toilet because I was so tired.
But this is later.
Now, I mean maybe perhaps 12 years old, what I'm just talking about.
But you asked about the religious.
Yes, my-- both parents were practicing, but Mother was much more, what I call today, reasonable.
For instance, I remember that I hated matzo.
I just didn't like that stuff.
And my father wouldn't allow anything, and I still remember my mother once secretly putting a little piece of bread in my hand and say, go and hide it.
Eat it.
Because I wasn't eating.
I was a very sickly child-- nobody will believe that-- and really thin.
[LAUGHS]
So she must have been worried, but she did that for me.
She was much more at ease, I always felt, with her religion.
If she felt something had to be done, it had to be done even-- by my father would not know about it.
And it made me-- now, I'm getting older, I'm getting 14, 15-- it made me very angry, that I didn't think he was honest about these things.
I, you know, I was imbued with honesty.
I'll tell you a little later about that.
But I felt, for instance, that why that it was so important that everything was, you know, exactly done the right way when he knew that his wife wasn't doing it always?
Why did he have to be so rigid about these things?
I didn't like that.
Now, I have to say, in terms of religion, I have was very religious.
I was also-- I'm getting to be 12 and 14 years old-- very interested in art.
I went to these wonderful Berlin museums and I saw all Christian art too.
I didn't say that it was anything wrong or that it was that religious.
It was beautiful.
But my Jewish colleagues in school thought I was terrible that I knew, meanwhile, who the apostles were.
I didn't become Christian.
I just thought it was beautiful.
This is a period-- aged 12, 13-- that I could talk probably for the rest of the time, which I will not do, that had enormous impact on my life.
It was art, it was reading, it were the revolutionary writers in Berlin.
It was impact my father too.
When I was eight years old, he took me into the Tiergarten and said, here are people who have killed Rosa Luxemburg-- L-U-X-E-M-B-U-R-G-- and Libknecht-- L-I-B-K-N-E-C-H-T.
Who were revolutionaries.
And he said, they killed them here.
Now, my father said, I didn't agree with them because they were for violence, and he was not.
He was a pacifist.
But still, one doesn't kill people for their opinion.
Now, this is much earlier.
This is age eight.
But age 12, 13, I begin to read all the literature, and I could name it, all the revolutionary literature.
I also started to read other things.
I can't think.
Who's the one who first-- Heckel, H-E-C-K-E-L-- who talked about development of the human beings, like Darwin.
And that was a little different.
I went always to Hebrew school.
I have learned a lot of Hebrew.
But-- and I was a good Hebrew teacher-- student, not-- my teachers liked me.
But I read all of that.
It was very exciting.
My older sister would hide the books.
She read them too, but she was afraid that Father would see it.
I became very you must be open, you must be honest, that kind of stuff.
I have to say, at that time entered something that probably has influenced me more in my life than anything else.
And that is the youth movement, Y-O-U-T-H, that too many people think is a Nazi thing.
No, it isn't at all.
The youth movement in Germany and Europe, not just in Germany, started around 1900.
I wasn't alive then.
But they were very idealistic young people.
They were, they were against all this lying and pretending.
They wore clothes that were different than their parents told them to wear.
The girls wouldn't wear a corset.
You know, these kind of things.
The boys wore open collars, which was, at that time, terrible.
You know, not the closed collar.
But they hiked.
Nature was very important.
Literature, art, were very important.
And especially important was what they called free thinking.
You allowed yourself to question everything.
I don't remember, it must be when I was 13 or so, entered a youth movement that was a Jewish youth movement, Kameraden.
K-A-M-E-R-A-D-E-N.
The person who got me into it, who later became a very prominent person in Israel, was Rosa Baline.
She wasn't called that in Israel, so I don't know any more.
But the Balines were rich Jews in Berlin.
But Rosa was very much for the poor, for everything.
But she got me into that, a wonderful, gentle person.
The youth movement at that time was, to me, the most important thing.
You-- there were all friends.
We were very close.
We believed in beauty.
We believed in justice.
We believed that poor people shouldn't be treated differently than rich people.
Everything that I stand for still, even today, it was there.
And we also were angry at some of the adults that would treat us like nothing.
And you know, many people know that all my life has been speaking up for young people.
You know, once somebody called me the mother of all delinquents.
And maybe that's what I am.
But it starts at that time.
I can tell for hours stories that I always say the dumb adults never remember, but I remember them.
We were not-- we were idealistic.
We wanted the world to be better.
We were not violent, but we wanted to be that we were recognized.
Part of the Kameraden became a split off.
It was called the Schwarzer Haufen, Schwarzer, black.
Whatever Haufen is.
I don't know.
H-A-U-F-E-N.
They were the radical part, the real radicals.
And we did question a lot of things that were taught in the synagogues.
And I myself at that-- now, I will tell something else in a minute, but I finish about the Schwarzer Haufen.

There were very wonderful people in it, Hans Litten, who later has been known all over the world as the first lawyer who asked Hitler to testify and therefore, was tortured, horribly in the worst way that anybody, and killed.
There's Max Furst, F-U-R-S-T, who was written about it.
There were other like that.
Oh, yes.
We were very, very close.
We were very close.
This is that whole movement.
But I wanted to say something that is at the same time almost.
And that is a cousin of mine came from Poland after the war.
He had been there once before the war.
His name was Henryk, H-E-N-R-Y-K, Glasser, G-L-A-S-S-E-R.
And I will make that very quick, I hope.
He was there when I was four years old, and I've never forgotten that he treated a four-year-old with respect.
And I was in love with him.
Then he went into the war.
And certainly, I always felt so terrible for all those who were in the war.
But he came after the war, and he came very ill.
There was a kidney illness that I don't know today what it was, but it was terminal.
And I was perhaps 14, 15.
I visited him every day in the hospital and read to him all the poetry that Toller, T-O-L-L-E-R, Schiller, or anybody wrote.
And to me, this was seeing somebody die that shouldn't die.
So I wrote poetry at that time already.
And I prayed to God that he mustn't let people who are good die.
There have been too many good people died.
And I would be a very religious person if he wouldn't let him die.
This is a child, in some ways, that we are.
And I remember I came home and Mother said, Henryk died.
And I ran out and said, what kind of a God is that?
I was very upset about that.
I sat shiva with them.
I cried.
But that was the beginning of questioning an awful lot.
And now come all this other intellectual stuff that I said before.
And then comes all the friends with whom we talk.
Is this really-- why are there different religions?
Why are they-- are they all believing in the same God?
Then I had a very close friend, who later also was killed by the Nazis, but her mother and she belonged to something that I had never even known that existed.
They didn't belong to any religion, and I didn't know that that's possible.
And they were very good people.
But oh, I cannot describe the debates we had, I, saying that well, Moses heard God, so it must be real.
And she says, no, it was just the thunder.
You know, these kind of thing.
So we have to remember that there is a lot of struggle in that period.
And I decided I would not go any more to the synagogue, especially not to high holy days, because people only went there to wear their good clothes.
Well, I think about 16.
And I had my own group.
I was already a leader at that time.
There are thousands of other things I could tell about, but my goodness, we will never get into anything.
But to say I said I wouldn't go.
My father was shocked, said he would forbid me to ever leave the house again.
Then I said it didn't matter to me, and I was crying.
We had really heavy fights.
And then he did something that I think was very smart.
He sent me to-- yeah.
He sent me to a non-Orthodox rabbi.
And that rabbi helped.
